elements.sent-2411-001: How can models effectively detect out-of-distribution (OOD) samples in complex, multi-label settings without extensive retraining? Existing OOD detection methods struggle to capture the intricate semantic relationships and label co-occurrences inherent in multi-label settings, often requiring large amounts of training data and failing to generalize to unseen label combinations.
elements.sent-2411-002: While large language models have revolutionized zero-shot OOD detection, they primarily focus on single-label scenarios, leaving a critical gap in handling real-world tasks where samples can be associated with multiple interdependent labels.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address these challenges, we introduce \method, a novel zero-shot multi-label OOD detection framework.
elements.sent-2411-004: By enriching the semantic space with both positive and negative concepts for each label, our approach models complex label dependencies, precisely differentiating OOD samples without the need for additional training.
elements.sent-2411-005: Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing approaches, achieving approximately 95\% average AUROC on both VOC and COCO datasets, while maintaining robust performance across varying numbers of labels and different types of OOD samples.
elements.sent-2411-006: As machine learning (ML) models become essential in a range of real-world applications, out-of-distribution (OOD) detection has gained increasing importance \cite{yang2024generalized .}
elements.sent-2411-007: OOD detection is particularly critical in fields such as autonomous driving \cite{elhafsi2023semantic , medical diagnostics \cite{huang2024adapting , and surveillance \cite{sultani2018real , where managing data that deviates from the training distribution—known as in-distribution (ID)—is necessary to avoid safety risks or incorrect decisions \cite{hendrycks2019scaling .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Thus, developing robust OOD detection techniques is key to ensuring model reliability in unpredictable environments.
elements.sent-2411-010: The rise of large models, particularly Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and MultiModal Large Language Models (MLLMs), has redefined the landscape of OOD detection \cite{xu2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Traditional OOD methods, such as Maximum Softmax Probability (MSP) and Mahalanobis distance-based techniques \cite{lee2018simple , typically require extensive task-specific training on in-distribution (ID) data.}
elements.sent-2411-012: In contrast, LLM-based methods leverage pre-trained knowledge, allowing for zero-shot and few-shot OOD detection capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-013: Models like CLIP \cite{radford2021learning exemplify this shift by achieving effective OOD detection with minimal task-specific training, relying on robust, pre-trained representations from multimodal datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Recent advancements, such as NegLabel \cite{jiang2024negative , refine zero-shot OOD detection by introducing negative mining strategies to identify semantically meaningful OOD categories.}
elements.sent-2411-015: This approach highlights that carefully selected negative labels can significantly improve detection performance without retraining.
elements.sent-2411-016: LM-OOD methods offer distinct advantages over traditional approaches, including strong performance in limited data, high adaptability across various tasks, and improved computational efficiency through reduced dependence on task-specific data preparation and retraining \cite{ming2022delving, miyai2024generalized, xu2024large .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/motivation_2.pdf} \caption{ Motivation for Concept-Based OOD Detection. Traditional methods struggle with complex multi-label cases. Our approach expands the label space with positive and negative concepts, enabling robust detection of complex OOD samples like ``Okapi" and ``Spork." } \label{fig:motivation}
elements.sent-2411-018: Large models for OOD detection are primarily designed for single-label tasks, where each input corresponds to a single, definitive label \cite{fort2021exploring, ming2022delving .}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, many real-world applications are inherently multi-label in nature: medical imaging often requires identifying multiple coexisting conditions \cite{kermany2018identifying, huang2024adapting .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Current state-of-the-art methods like NegLabel \cite{jiang2024negative encounter limitations in these multi-label contexts, as seen in Fig.~\ref{fig:motivation : First, the complexity of semantic similarity computations increases significantly with multiple concurrent labels compared to single labels.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Second, these methods assume OOD samples are semantically distant from ID classes, which may not hold in multi-label settings where novel combinations of known concepts could form valid OOD cases \cite{wang2023clipn, nie2024out .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Additionally, these approaches struggle to effectively model complex label co-occurrence patterns and conditional dependencies, both crucial in multi-label contexts \cite{zhang2014review, yu2014large .}
elements.sent-2411-023: These limitations highlight an essential gap in adapting LM-OOD methods for multi-label tasks, where it is necessary to differentiate between known and unknown label combinations within complex label spaces for practical application \cite{miyai2024generalized, yang2024generalized .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Our Proposal: Extending LM-OOD to Multi-Label Settings.
elements.sent-2411-025: In this work, we introduce \method (concept-based OOD detection), the first multi-label OOD detection framework that leverages VLM models, specifically CLIP \cite{wang2023clipn , in a zero-shot setting.}
elements.sent-2411-026: It enriches the base label set with two fine-grained concepts: positive concepts, which capture complex semantic details closely related to ID classes, and negative concepts, which, filtered by a similarity threshold, introduce semantically distant features to strengthen ID-OOD separation.
elements.sent-2411-027: By leveraging CLIP’s extensive pre-trained multimodal knowledge, \method achieves robust and flexible OOD detection in complex, multi-label scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our technical contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-029: Novel Multi-label OOD Detection: We present a novel multi-label OOD detection framework based on the CLIP to achieve zero-shot detection without additional training.
elements.sent-2411-030: Concept-based Label Expansion: We introduce a concept-based label expansion that leverages positive and negative concepts for precise discrimination of OOD samples and provide interpretability in multi-label tasks.
elements.sent-2411-031: Effectiveness: Our method demonstrates superior performance across standard benchmarks, outperforming existing methods in multi-label OOD detection while maintaining efficiency and robustness in real-world settings.
elements.sent-2411-032: Our method achieves an OOD sample detection efficiency of approximately 800 images per second on the CLIP-B/16.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width = \textwidth]{figures/updated_figure.pdf} \caption{ Overview of \method (\S \ref{subsec:overview} \tikzsquare[stepOneColor, fill=stepOneColor]{3pt}). The Concept Generation module (\S \ref{subsec:concept}) uses LLMs to expand base labels into positive ($\mathcal{P}$) and negative ($\mathcal{N}$) concept sets, enhancing the ID-OOD boundary. Positive concepts capture fine-grained, ID-aligned features, while negative concepts provide contrasting OOD-aligned features. The Similarity and ID Score Computation module (\S \ref{subsec:score} \tikzsquare[stepThreeColor, fill=stepThreeColor]{3pt}) encodes an input image and computes similarity scores with $\mathcal{P}$, $\mathcal{B}$ (base), and $\mathcal{N}$ sets. An ID score based on top-$k$ similarities then classifies the image for ID/OOD. } \label{fig:method}
elements.sent-2411-033: In multi-label OOD detection, we aim to determine whether a given input image $I \in \mathcal{I}$ belongs to the ID or represents an unknown OOD sample.
elements.sent-2411-034: We denote the set of base labels as $\mathcal{B = \{\ell_1, \ell_2, \dots, \ell_{|\mathcal{B |}\}$, representing known classes within the ID.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Each image $I$ can be associated with multiple labels from $\mathcal{B}$, capturing the multi-label nature of this task.
elements.sent-2411-036: To formalize this, we define a scoring function, $S_{\text{ID : \mathcal{I \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, which assigns an ID score to each image.}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: For simplicity, we frame our approach around this ID score rather than the OOD score, with the following criteria: \begin{equation}S_{\text{ID}}(I) \begin{cases} > \gamma, \text{if } I \sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{ID}}, \leq \gamma, \text{if } I \sim \mathcal{D}_{\text{OOD}}, \end{cases}\end{equation} where $\gamma$ is a threshold determined through validation, and $\mathcal{D _{\text{ID }$ and $\mathcal{D _{\text{OOD }$ are ID and OOD distributions, respectively.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}S_{ID}(I)  > \gamma, if  I \sim D_{ID}, \leq \gamma, if  I \sim D_{OOD}, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-038: In this setup, $S_{\text{ID }$ provides a measure of similarity to the ID distribution, aiding in distinguishing between known and unknown samples in a multi-label context.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) bring powerful multimodal capabilities for OOD detection, allowing more flexible, context-aware identification of OOD samples \cite{miyai2024generalized, xu2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Large-scale pre-trained VLMs, such as CLIP \cite{radford2021learning and GPT-4V \cite{zhang2023exploring , enable models to process images and text prompts together, enhancing adaptability and precision across diverse visual and textual domains \cite{fort2021exploring .}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: VLM-based OOD detection generally follows two primary approaches \cite{xu2024large : Prompting-based Detection, which directly prompts VLMs to respond with OOD indicators \cite{cao2023towards , and Contrasting-based Detection, which uses multimodal VLMs pre-trained with contrastive objectives to distinguish OOD samples \cite{ming2022delving .}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: This work focuses on the contrasting-based approach in multimodal contexts, as it is well-suited for enhancing OOD detection by amplifying distinctions between ID and OOD classes.
elements.sent-2411-043: Methods like NegLabel \cite{jiang2024negative and NegPrompt \cite{li2024learning fall under this category.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: NegLabel enhances OOD detection by introducing negative labels to contrast with in-distribution (ID) classes, while NegPrompt uses learned negative prompts to emphasize OOD differences by contrasting them with ID prompts.. Formal Definition.
elements.sent-2411-045: The OOD detection score \( S(x) \) for a sample \( x \) is defined as: \begin{small \begin{equation}S_{OOD}(x) = \frac{\sum_{i \in Y} \exp\big(\text{sim}(h, e_i)\big)}{\sum_{i \in Y} \exp\big(\text{sim}(h, e_i)\big) + \sum_{j \in Y^-} \exp\big(\text{sim}(h, e_j^-)\big)},\end{equation} \end{small where \( h \) is the embedding of \( x \); \( e_i \) and \( e_j^- \) are embeddings for ID and negative representations (NegPrompt \cite{li2024learning or NegLabel\cite{jiang2024negative ) from sets \( Y \) and \( Y^- \), respectively; and \( \text{sim (h, e) \) denotes the similarity (e.g., cosine similarity) between embeddings.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}S_{OOD}(x) = \frac{\sum_{i \in Y} \exp\big(sim(h, e_i)\big)}{\sum_{i \in Y} \exp\big(sim(h, e_i)\big) + \sum_{j \in Y^-} \exp\big(sim(h, e_j^-)\big)},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-046: The numerator measures similarity to ID representations, while the denominator incorporates both ID and negative similarities, amplifying the detection signal.
elements.sent-2411-047: Note that, our definition is a little different, a smaller $S(x)$ means a larger probability of $x$ being an OOD sample.
elements.sent-2411-049: Current methods like NegLabel and NegPrompt assume that OOD classes are semantically distant from ID classes, which may not hold in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-050: OOD samples can closely resemble ID classes, particularly in fine-grained distinctions (e.g., similar dog breeds), limiting the effectiveness of a purely semantic distance-based approach.
elements.sent-2411-051: Moreover, extending single-label OOD detection methods \cite{sun2021react, liu2020energy to multi-label contexts presents additional challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Multi-label settings often involve significant semantic overlap between labels and co-occurring ID and OOD labels, complicating the extraction of effective negative labels or prompts.
elements.sent-2411-053: These issues highlight the need for advanced detection techniques to capture the complex dependencies and relationships inherent in multi-label OOD detection tasks.
elements.sent-2411-054: The \method framework improves OOD detection by refining the decision boundary between ID and OOD samples using fine-grained concepts.
elements.sent-2411-055: By introducing positive and negative concept sets, $\mathcal{P}$ and $\mathcal{N}$, \method leverages LLMs to add contextual information around ID samples, thereby enhancing sensitivity to OOD cases.
elements.sent-2411-056: Concept Generation and Similarity Measure (\S \ref{subsec:concept ): To distinguish ID from OOD samples, we generate positive concepts ($\mathcal{P}$) and negative concepts ($\mathcal{N}$) that are closely and distantly related to ID classes, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Positive concepts, mined using LLMs, capture domain-relevant features of ID samples at a fine-grained level, while negative concepts are chosen based on their semantic distance from ID classes to enhance contrast.
elements.sent-2411-058: Similarity and ID Score Computation (\S \ref{subsec:score ): For each input image \( I \), we compute similarity scores with these concept sets and the base label set $\mathcal{B}$, forming a robust semantic space for evaluating ID-OOD relationships.}
elements.sent-2411-059: After this, we define an ID score \( S_{\text{ID (I) \) that aggregates the image’s alignment with ID concepts ($\mathcal{B}$ and $\mathcal{P}$) and contrasts it with the negative concepts ($\mathcal{N}$).}}
elements.sent-2411-060: When this score falls below a predefined threshold \( \gamma \), the image is classified as OOD.
elements.sent-2411-061: This scoring method sharpens the decision boundary by leveraging contrasts between positive and negative concepts, giving more accurate OOD detection.
elements.sent-2411-062: The pseudocode in Algo.~\ref{alg:ood_detection outlines the full procedure for \method.}
elements.sent-2411-063: To formalize the OOD detection decision, we compute an ID score and classify an image \( X \) as OOD if this score falls below a threshold \( \gamma \).
elements.sent-2411-064: Specifically, the decision function \( \tilde{Y \) is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \tilde{Y} = \mathbb{I}(S_{\text{ID}}(h, P, N) < \gamma), \quad \text{where} \quad h = f_{\text{img}}(X), P = f_{\text{text}}(\text{prompt}(\mathcal{P})), \quad N = f_{\text{text}}(\text{prompt}(\mathcal{N})). \end{aligned}\end{equation} Here, \( h \) is the image embedding for \( X \), obtained using an image encoder \( f_{\text{img \), while \( P \) and \( N \) are the positive and negative concept embeddings generated via text prompts for concept sets \( \mathcal{P \) and \( \mathcal{N \), respectively.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation} \tilde{Y} = I(S_{ID}(h, P, N) < \gamma), \quad where \quad h = f_{img}(X), P = f_{text}(prompt(P)), \quad N = f_{text}(prompt(N)). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-065: The indicator function \( \mathbb{I \) produces the final OOD classification.. Theoretical Justification.}
elements.sent-2411-066: The proposed \method approach is computationally efficient, leveraging pre-trained embeddings without requiring additional model training.
elements.sent-2411-067: By incorporating positive and negative concepts, the model gains enhanced semantic understanding, allowing for clearer differentiation between ID and OOD samples.
elements.sent-2411-068: The focus on top-$k$ similarity values makes the method robust to noise, ensuring stable performance across varied datasets and label sets.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Multi-Label OOD Detection with \method} \label{alg:ood_detection} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Image $I$, base label set $\mathcal{B}$, threshold $\gamma$, top-$k$ parameter $k$ \ENSURE Classification of $I$ as ID or OOD \STATE Concept Generation (\S \ref{subsec:concept}): Generate positive concepts $\mathcal{P}$ and negative concepts $\mathcal{N}$ using an LLM. \STATE Embedding Computation: Compute embeddings for $I$, as well as for each label in $\mathcal{B}$, $\mathcal{P}$, and $\mathcal{N}$. \STATE Top-$k$ Similarity Calculation (\S \ref{subsec:score}): Calculate the top-$k$ mean similarity scores, $\mu_k(\mathcal{B}, I)$, $\mu_k(\mathcal{P}, I)$, and $\mu_k(\mathcal{N}, I)$. \STATE ID Score Computation (\S \ref{subsec:score}): Using the top-$k$ similarities, compute the ID score $S_{\text{ID}}(I)$ according to Eq.~\eqref{eq:ood_score}. \STATE Decision: if $S_{\text{ID}}(I) > \gamma$ then classify $I$ as ID; else classify $I$ as OOD. \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-069: The concept generation process in \method creates a rich semantic space to strengthen ID-OOD distinctions.
elements.sent-2411-070: By constructing a set of positive concepts, $\mathcal{P}$, closely aligned with ID samples, and a set of negative concepts, $\mathcal{N}$, that enhances the contrast with OOD samples, we can refine the decision boundary between ID and OOD.
elements.sent-2411-072: To build a comprehensive set of positive concepts that accurately represents ID characteristics, we use a two-stage approach inspired by LF-CBM \cite{lf-cbm .}
elements.sent-2411-073: This process enriches each base label by capturing specific attributes that reinforce its identity within the ID class.
elements.sent-2411-074: Concept Querying: We prompt GPT-4 to generate concepts in three distinct contexts for each target object, using structured prompts tailored to elicit three categories: features (prompt$_F$), superclasses (prompt$_S$), and commonly associated items (prompt$_C$).
elements.sent-2411-075: This contextual querying ensures that each concept captures detailed, complex information, improving the clarity and consistency of the concept pool.
elements.sent-2411-076: Each prompt is crafted to encourage concise, relevant responses without qualifiers, which enhances precision, detailed in the Appendix \ref{appx:concept_generation .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Concept Filtering: After generating these candidate concepts, we apply filtering criteria to retain only the most distinctive and relevant features.
elements.sent-2411-078: This step is essential to ensure that the concept set effectively captures the ID characteristics required to differentiate ID from OOD.
elements.sent-2411-079: Our filtering process, detailed in the Appendix \ref{appx:concept_generation , refines the generated concepts into a cohesive and meaningful set of positive labels.}
elements.sent-2411-080: The final positive concept set, $\mathcal{P}$, is constructed as: \begin{small \begin{equation}\mathcal{P} = \bigcup_{c_i \in \mathcal{B}} \left( \text{prompt}_{F}(c_i) \cup \text{prompt}_{S}(c_i) \cup \text{prompt}_{C}(c_i) \right),\end{equation} \end{small where $\mathcal{B}$ denotes the set of ID labels, and each $c_i \in \mathcal{B}$ is queried to generate its positive concept labels.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: To reinforce the distinction between ID and OOD samples, we develop a set of negative concepts, $\mathcal{N}$, using a process called NegMining \cite{jiang2024negative .}
elements.sent-2411-083: This approach helps us identify concepts that are semantically distant from ID labels, forming a contrasting boundary that enhances OOD detection.
elements.sent-2411-084: Starting with a large collection of words from a lexical database like WordNet, we create a candidate label space \( \mathcal{N ^c = \{n_1, n_2, \dots, n_C\} \).}
elements.sent-2411-085: For each candidate label \( \tilde{n _i \) in this set, we calculate a similarity score based on its cosine similarity with the entire ID label set, ensuring that the chosen negative concepts have minimal alignment with ID labels.}
elements.sent-2411-086: For each ID label \( l \in \mathcal{B \), we obtain its text embedding \( e_l \) using a text encoder \( f^{\text{text \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: For each candidate \( \tilde{n _i \in \mathcal{N ^c \), we compute its embedding: \begin{equation}\tilde{e}_i = f^{\text{text}}(\text{prompt}(\tilde{n}_i)).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: We select the top candidates with the smallest similarity scores relative to the ID label set, forming a set of negative labels that are maximally distant from ID concepts.
elements.sent-2411-089: The final negative concept set is defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{N} = \left\{ n \mid \text{Sim}(\tilde{n}_i, \mathcal{B}) < \tau_{i} \right\}, \tau_i = \text{percentile}_\eta \left( \{\text{Sim}(\tilde{e}_i, e_l)\}_{l \in \mathcal{B}} \right), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \( \text{Sim (\tilde{n _i, \mathcal{B ) \) represents the similarity between candidate \( \tilde{n _i \) and the ID set \( \mathcal{B \), and \( \tau_i \) is the \( \eta \)-th percentile of similarity scores, providing robustness against outliers.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation} N = \left\{ n \mid Sim(\tilde{n}_i, B) < \tau_{i} \right\}, \tau_i = percentile_\eta \left( \{Sim(\tilde{e}_i, e_l)\}_{l \in B} \right), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-090: By expanding the base label set $\mathcal{B}$ to include positive concepts $\mathcal{P}$ and contrasting them with negative concepts $\mathcal{N}$, we create an enriched semantic space.
elements.sent-2411-091: This structure allows for more precise discrimination between ID and OOD samples, capturing both the core characteristics of the ID classes and their semantic boundaries effectively.
elements.sent-2411-092: After generating our positive and negative concepts, \method computes an ID score for each input image \( I \), based on its similarity with the positive concept set \( \mathcal{P \), negative concept set \( \mathcal{N \), and base labels \( \mathcal{B \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: This score helps determine if the image aligns more closely with ID or OOD characteristics.. ID Score Definition.
elements.sent-2411-094: The ID score \( S_{\text{ID (I) \) for an image \( I \) is: \begin{equation}\label{eq:ood_score} S_{\text{ID}}(I) = w_{\mathcal{B}} \cdot \mu_k(\mathcal{B}, I) - w_{\mathcal{P}} \cdot \mu_k(\mathcal{P}, I) - w_{\mathcal{N}} \cdot \mu_k(\mathcal{N}, I)\end{equation} where \( w_{\mathcal{B , w_{\mathcal{P , \) and \( w_{\mathcal{N \) are weighting coefficients that determine the relative contribution of each set in computing the ID score, reflecting the importance of the base labels \( \mathcal{B \), positive concepts \( \mathcal{P \), and negative concepts \( \mathcal{N \).}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{eq:ood_score} S_{ID}(I) = w_{B} \cdot \mu_k(B, I) - w_{P} \cdot \mu_k(P, I) - w_{N} \cdot \mu_k(N, I)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-096: The term \( \mu_k(\mathcal{S , I) \) in Eq. (\ref{eq:ood_score ) represents the top-\(k\) mean similarity score between the image \( I \) and the label set \( \mathcal{S \), calculated as: \begin{equation}\mu_k(\mathcal{S}, I) = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^k \text{sim}(h, e_i)\end{equation} where \( h \) is the embedding of image \( I \), and \( e_i \) are the embeddings of the top-\(k\) closest elements in set \( \mathcal{S \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Here, \( \mathcal{S \) can be any of \(\mathcal{B}\) (base labels), \(\mathcal{P}\) (positive concepts), or \(\mathcal{N}\) (negative concepts).}
elements.sent-2411-098: The similarity function \( \text{sim (h, e_i) \) (e.g., cosine similarity) captures the alignment between the image embedding \( h \) and each of the concept embeddings \( e_i \).}
elements.sent-2411-099: This top-\(k\) similarity measure emphasizes the most relevant matches between the image and the concepts in each set, minimizing the influence of less relevant or noisy features and enhancing robustness in scoring.
elements.sent-2411-101: The scoring function \( S_{\text{ID (I) \) captures the degree of alignment of an image with ID or OOD characteristics.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: For ID samples, we anticipate high alignment with both the base labels \( \mathcal{B \) and positive concepts \( \mathcal{P \), yielding a high \( S_{\text{ID (I) \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Conversely, OOD samples will likely align more closely with negative concepts \( \mathcal{N \), resulting in a lower ID score.}
elements.sent-2411-104: By focusing on the top-\(k\) similarities, this approach enhances robustness to noise and emphasizes the most semantically relevant features.
elements.sent-2411-105: This mechanism refines the boundary between ID and OOD samples by leveraging positive and negative contrasts, resulting in more accurate and reliable OOD detection.
elements.sent-2411-106: We assume the following bounds on the similarity scores \(\mu_k(\cdot, \cdot)\) between an image \(I\) and class representations in different contexts when \(I\) is either ID or OOD: \begin{equation}0 \leq \mu_k(B, I_{OOD}) \leq a < \mu_k(B, I_{ID}) \leq A \leq 1,\end{equation} \begin{equation}0 \leq \mu_k(P, I_{OOD}) \leq b < \mu_k(P, I_{ID}) \leq B \leq 1,\end{equation} \begin{equation}0 \leq \mu_k(N, I_{ID}) \leq c < \mu_k(N, I_{OOD}) \leq C \leq 1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-107: To establish separability between ID and OOD samples, we examine bounds on the scoring function for in-distribution and out-of-distribution images.. 1.
elements.sent-2411-108: Lower Bound for \(S_{\text{ID}\): Based on the assumed bounds, the lower bound of the scoring function \(S_{\text{ID }\) for ID samples is: $S_{\text{ID ^{\text{lower = w_B a - w_P B - w_{\mathcal{N c$.. 2.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Upper Bound for \(S_{\text{ID}\): The upper bound of the scoring function \(S_{\text{ID }\) for OOD samples is: $S_{\text{ID ^{\text{upper = w_B a - w_{\mathcal{N C$.. 3.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Separability Condition: For the scoring function to effectively distinguish between ID and OOD samples, we require: $S_{\text{ID ^{\text{lower - S_{\text{ID ^{\text{upper > 0$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Substituting the expressions for \(S_{\text{ID ^{\text{lower }\) and \(S_{\text{ID ^{\text{upper }\), this simplifies to: $w_P B < w_{\mathcal{N (C - c)$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: By selecting \(w_P\) and \(w_{\mathcal{N }\) to satisfy this condition, we ensure that the scoring function \(S_{\text{ID }\) can differentiate between ID and OOD samples based on semantic similarity.}}
elements.sent-2411-113: This lemma establishes a theoretical basis for using the scoring function to distinguish ID from OOD samples by focusing on top-$k$ similarities, enhancing robustness by minimizing the effect of weakly relevant labels.

elements.sent-2411-115: We evaluate our proposed multi-label OOD detection method on widely used datasets.
elements.sent-2411-116: For ID datasets, we use Pascal VOC \cite{everingham2010pascal , MS-COCO \cite{coco , and Objects365 \cite{shao2019objects365 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: For OOD datasets, we use Textures \cite{cimpoi14describing and Filtered ImageNet22K \cite{hendrycks2019benchmark .}}
elements.sent-2411-118: These datasets provide diverse categories and rich multi-label annotations, making them ideal for assessing OOD detection in complex real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-119: To quantify the OOD detection performance, we report two standard metrics, FPR@95 and AUROC.. See Appx.
elements.sent-2411-120: Our method is implemented using PyTorch and evaluated on NVIDIA 3090 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-121: As a zero-shot approach, our method leverages pre-computed concept embeddings and does not require any additional training.
elements.sent-2411-122: This results in a computationally efficient solution, with an average inference time of approximately 800 images per second.
elements.sent-2411-123: This efficient design allows for parallelized OOD detection alongside zero-shot classification tasks, making it suitable for deployment in real-time applications.
elements.sent-2411-124: For reproducibility, all hyperparameters and settings follow standard configurations, and further implementation details are provided in Appx.. \ref{appx:implementation .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width = \textwidth]{figures/explain.pdf} \caption{Interpretability analysis of \method on ID (left) and OOD (right) examples. For ID sample (dogs), positive concepts (e.g.,``four-legged creature") receive high weights, confirming ID alignment. For OOD samples (mosquito), negative concepts dominate.} \label{fig:explain}
elements.sent-2411-126: Table \ref{table:main presents a comparison of the OOD detection performance of \method against several baseline approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Where * represents our reproduction of the missing results from the original paper, and \dag means that we used the values from the original paper, and \dag * represents the best value we take from the replicated results and the reported results in the original paper.
elements.sent-2411-128: The gray boxes represent our approach.
elements.sent-2411-129: In addition, (Joint)-Energy denotes the better of the JointEnergy \citep{wang2021can method and the Energy \citep{liu2020energy method.}}
elements.sent-2411-130: Bold values indicate the best results.
elements.sent-2411-131: Our method demonstrates strong OOD detection performance across both ResNet-based and ViT-based architectures.
elements.sent-2411-132: Specifically, our ResNet-based CLIP achieves superior results, particularly on the Pascal VOC ID dataset and Textures OOD dataset, with FPR95 of 8.76\% and AUROC of 97.79\%, outperforming all baseline methods.
elements.sent-2411-133: These suggest that the ResNet-based architecture may be particularly effective in handling texture-oriented datasets.
elements.sent-2411-134: On the ViT-based CLIP, our method achieves the lowest FPR95 of 23.87\% on the Pascal VOC dataset and outperforms competing methods on the COCO dataset, achieving an impressive AUROC of 95.07\%.
elements.sent-2411-135: The ViT-based approach performs exceptionally well across most datasets, indicating that the ViT architecture enhances OOD detection across diverse image distributions, while still maintaining strong robustness on texture-based datasets.
elements.sent-2411-136: Importantly, our approach operates as a zero-shot method, requiring no additional training or complex parameter tuning.
elements.sent-2411-137: This makes it highly efficient with low computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-138: This contrasts with other multi-label classifiers requires training.
elements.sent-2411-139: For method without training, such as SeTAR \cite{li2024setar , which requires extensive parameter search time, impacting practical deployment.}
elements.sent-2411-140: Overall, our method provides an efficient and effective zero-shot solution for OOD detection, yielding state-of-the-art performance with minimal overhead on both architectures.
elements.sent-2411-141: Figure \ref{fig:explain presents a detailed interpretability analysis of \method through a t-SNE visualization \cite{van2008visualizing of text embeddings and several qualitative examples.}}
elements.sent-2411-142: The interpretability of \method is a key feature, as it allows us to visualize and understand how specific concepts contribute to OOD detection.. t-SNE Visualization.
elements.sent-2411-143: The t-SNE plot on the left side of Fig. \ref{fig:explain visualizes the embeddings of concepts from our model, showing a clear separation between ID labels (Pascal VOC, shown in red) and OOD concepts.}
elements.sent-2411-144: Notably, the embeddings for positive concepts (shown in red) form distinct clusters separate from negative concepts (shown in blue).
elements.sent-2411-145: This clustering reflects the model's ability to encode meaningful semantic information that distinguishes ID from OOD samples.
elements.sent-2411-146: The clustering of positive concepts around ID points and the scattering of negative concepts in more distant regions confirm that the model effectively captures relationships that contribute to its decision-making process.. Qualitative Examples.
elements.sent-2411-147: The right side of Fig. \ref{fig:tsne provides several example images with corresponding bar plots, illustrating the concept weights associated with each image.}
elements.sent-2411-148: In each bar plot, positive concepts are marked in red, while negative concepts are marked in blue.
elements.sent-2411-149: This visual representation offers insight into which semantic features are influencing the OOD score, making it easier to interpret why certain samples are classified as ID or OOD.
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{Ablations on Pascal VOC and COCO datasets. Lower top-k values (e.g., 0.1, 0.2) improve performance by focusing on key concepts. Using both $S_{\text{A}}$ and $S_{\text{B}}$ together yields the best results, with an FPR95 of 24.78\% and AUROC of 94.27\% on Pascal VOC. } \label{table:ablation} 0.9{ \begin{tabular}{ccc|cc|cc} Top-$k$ $S_{\text{A}}$ $S_{\text{B}}$ \multicolumn{2}{c|}{Pascal VOC ID} \multicolumn{2}{c}{COCO ID} FPR95 AUROC FPR95 AUROC 0.05 \xmark \cmark 39.39 89.08 26.65 94.40 0.2 \xmark \cmark 36.12 91.72 48.87 89.49 0.05 \cmark \xmark 45.79 90.13 28.88 92.39 0.2 \cmark \xmark 36.43 91.73 33.70 90.09 0.05 \cmark \cmark 36.28 91.28 20.37 95.07 0.1 \cmark \cmark 28.07 93.60 24.16 93.91 0.2 \cmark \cmark 24.78 94.27 29.08 92.39 0.3 \cmark \cmark 26.76 93.62 32.95 91.02 0.5 \cmark \cmark 32.23 91.82 39.61 88.48 \end{tabular} }
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Representative OOD Detection Performance on Pascal VOC (Textures) with Different Architectures and Distance Metrics} \label{table:representative_results} \begin{tabular}{llSS} Architecture Distance Metric FPR95 AUROC ResNet50 mean 8.76 97.79 ResNet50x16 mean 18.54 95.89 ViT-B/16 mean 21.58 95.14 ViT-B/16 percentile (0.75) 26.62 93.99 ViT-L/14 mean 24.28 94.55 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-150: To assess the contributions of different components in our approach, we conduct an ablation study with variations in the parameters top-k, $S_{\text{A }$, and $S_{\text{B }$ on the Pascal VOC and COCO datasets, as shown in Table \ref{table:ablation .}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: For more experiments on different model architectures and OOD score functions, please refer to the Appendix \ref{appx:exp .}
elements.sent-2411-152: Recall that we defined the score function in Eq. (\ref{eq:ood_score ).}
elements.sent-2411-153: Here, we simplify the score function by letting $w_{\mathcal{B }$ to 2, and $w_{\mathcal{P }$ and $w_{\mathcal{N }$ to 1 and let Score Components ($S_{\text{A}$ and $S_{\text{B }$) correspond to the two terms in the simplified scoring function: \begin{small \begin{equation}S_{\text{OOD}}(I) = \underbracket{\left[ \mu_k(\mathcal{B}, I) - \mu_k(\mathcal{P}, I) \right]}_{S_{\text{A}}} + \underbracket{\left[ \mu_k(\mathcal{B}, I) - \mu_k(\mathcal{N}, I) \right]}_{S_{\text{B}}}\end{equation} \end{small Effect of Top-k Selection.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: The top-k parameter controls the number of concepts used in similarity calculations, allowing us to focus on the most relevant information.
elements.sent-2411-155: Lower values of top-k (e.g., 0.1 and 0.2) generally yield better results, as they prioritize the top-matching concepts, minimizing noise from less related features.
elements.sent-2411-156: For instance, with top-k set to 0.1, our approach achieves an FPR95 of 28.07\% and an AUROC of 93.6\% on Pascal VOC, outperforming larger top-k values which include more potentially distracting concepts.
elements.sent-2411-158: Enabling both $S_{\text{A }$ and $S_{\text{B }$ yields the best performance across metrics.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: For example, on Pascal VOC, activating both scores reduces FPR95 from 36.12\% to 24.78\% and boosts AUROC from 91.72\% to 94.27\%.
elements.sent-2411-160: This improvement indicates that $S_{\text{A }$ and $S_{\text{B }$ capture complementary aspects of the data, thereby enhancing the model's ability to differentiate between ID and OOD samples effectively.}}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width = 0.5 \textwidth, trim={15 15 15 15}]{figures/tsne.pdf} \caption{t-SNE plot for text embeddings. This clustering reflects the model's ability to encode meaningful semantic information that distinguishes ID labels, concepts and negative labels. } \label{fig:tsne}
elements.sent-2411-161: With the rise of contrastive learning-based cross-modal models like CLIP, traditional OOD detection methods face new challenges, particularly in zero-shot settings.
elements.sent-2411-162: In addition, the increasing complexity of image understanding tasks has introduced challenges in multi-label OOD detection.
elements.sent-2411-163: For OOD detection in CLIP-based settings, several methods have been proposed, including both zero-shot and fine-tuning approaches.
elements.sent-2411-164: For example, ZOC \citep{esmaeilpour2022zero introduces a trainable generator and additional data to create extra OOD labels.}
elements.sent-2411-165: Maximum Concept Matching (MCM) \citep{ming2022delving uses textual embeddings of ID classes as concept prototypes, calculating an MCM score based on the cosine similarity between image and textual features to identify OOD instances.}
elements.sent-2411-166: NegLabel \citep{jiang2024negative enhances OOD detection by incorporating a large set of negative labels, which are semantically distant from in-distribution (ID) labels, thereby improving the model’s ability to distinguish OOD samples.}
elements.sent-2411-167: GL-MCM \citep{miyai2023zero builds upon CLIP by considering both global and local visual-text alignments in the detection process.}
elements.sent-2411-168: SeTAR \citep{li2024setar applies selective low-rank approximation to weight matrices with a greedy search algorithm for optimized OOD detection.}
elements.sent-2411-169: NegPrompt \citep{nie2024out introduces transferable negative prompts for OOD detection, where each ID class is paired with learnable negative prompts, improving OOD detection by associating OOD samples more closely with negative prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-170: However, these approaches lack a discussion of multi-label scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-171: In multi-label tasks, some researchers have explored OOD detection as well.
elements.sent-2411-172: The JointEnergy-based method \citep{wang2021can is one of the first to address OOD detection in multi-label scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-173: A later improvement \citep{mei2024multi further refines this approach.}
elements.sent-2411-174: Additionally, YolOOD \citep{zolfi2024yolood explores OOD detection in multi-label tasks, using an object detection model as the backbone.}
elements.sent-2411-175: However, these methods do not explore CLIP-based architectures.
elements.sent-2411-176: Methods utilizing multi-label data with CLIP, such as GL-MCM \citep{miyai2023zero and SeTAR \citep{li2024setar , typically focus on curated datasets and fail to address the challenge of detecting OOD samples when multiple classes co-exist in the same image.}}
elements.sent-2411-177: In contrast, our work is the first to comprehensively explore how to effectively perform zero-shot OOD detection in multi-label tasks using CLIP models.
elements.sent-2411-178: Concept bottlenecks have emerged as a promising approach to improve model interpretability and robustness by explicitly modeling the intermediate concepts that influence predictions.
elements.sent-2411-179: Instead, it employs unsupervised methods to automatically identify and extract meaningful concepts from the data.
elements.sent-2411-180: Probabilistic Concept Bottleneck Models (ProbCBMs) \citep{kim2023probabilistic integrate uncertainty estimation with concept predictions.}
elements.sent-2411-181: Recently, various other CBM variants have emerged, expanding the versatility of this approach.
elements.sent-2411-182: While these methods have been effective in extracting concepts and describing multiple labels within images, to the best of our knowledge, none have directly applied these concept-based techniques to multi-label and complex OOD detection tasks.
elements.sent-2411-183: Our approach focuses on the novel application of concept bottlenecks specifically for improving OOD detection in such settings.
elements.sent-2411-184: This work presents an in-depth exploration of using the CLIP model for efficient zero-shot OOD detection in complex multi-label scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-185: Our concept-based approach demonstrates superior OOD detection performance, achieving over 95\% average AUROC on VOC and COCO (as ID datasets) with ImageNet and Texture as OOD datasets.
elements.sent-2411-186: Additionally, we conducted extensive experiments to evaluate the explainability and parameter sensitivity of the method.
elements.sent-2411-187: Our approach relies on the quality of concept generation and the robustness of embeddings from the CLIP model.
elements.sent-2411-188: Future work could investigate adaptive concept generation methods and alternative embedding models to enhance OOD detection performance in complex multi-label environments.
elements.sent-2411-189: Broader Impact Statement: \method enhances multi-label OOD detection, focusing on fine-grained concept differentiation to improve model reliability in applications like healthcare, autonomous systems, and security.
elements.sent-2411-190: By increasing robustness to unknown data, \method supports safer, more reliable performance in dynamic, high-stakes environments, reducing risks tied to novel or evolving data patterns.
elements.sent-2411-191: After receiving paper reviews, authors may optionally submit a rebuttal to address the reviewers' comments, which will be limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-192: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-193: The author rebuttal is optional and, following similar guidelines to previous conferences, is meant to provide you with an opportunity to rebut factual errors or to supply additional information requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-194: It is NOT intended to add new contributions (theorems, algorithms, experiments) that were absent in the original submission and NOT specifically requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-195: You may optionally add a figure, graph, or proof to your rebuttal to better illustrate your answer to the reviewers' comments.
elements.sent-2411-196: Per a passed 2018 PAMI-TC motion, reviewers should refrain from requesting significant additional experiments for the rebuttal or penalize for lack of additional experiments.
elements.sent-2411-197: Authors should refrain from including new experimental results in the rebuttal, especially when not specifically requested to do so by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-198: Authors may include figures with illustrations or comparison tables of results reported in the submission/supplemental material or in other papers.
elements.sent-2411-199: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-200: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-201: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-202: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-203: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-204: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-205: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-206: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-207: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-208: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-209: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-211: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-212: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-213: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-214: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-215: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-216: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in \cref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-217: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-218: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-219: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.
elements.figure-2411-008: \fbox{\rule{0pt}{0.5in} \rule{0.9\linewidth}{0pt}} \caption{Example of caption. It is set in Roman so that mathematics (always set in Roman: $B \sin A = A \sin B$) may be included without an ugly clash.} \label{fig:onecol}
elements.sent-2411-220: To avoid ambiguities, it is best if the numbering for equations, figures, tables, and references in the author response does not overlap with that in the main paper (the reviewer may wonder if you talk about \cref{fig:onecol in the author response or in the paper).}
elements.sent-2411-223: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
elements.sent-2411-224: Resize fonts in figures to match the font in the body text, and choose line widths which render effectively in print.
elements.sent-2411-225: Readers (and reviewers), even of an electronic copy, may choose to print your response in order to read it.
elements.sent-2411-226: You cannot insist that they do otherwise, and therefore must not assume that they can zoom in to see tiny details on a graphic.
elements.sent-2411-227: When placing figures in \LaTeX, it is almost always best to use \verb+\includegraphics+, and to specify the figure width as a multiple of the line width as in the example below { .}
